findings: The hero headline adds a multicolor rocket emoji after the pink emphasis word, introducing extra highlight colors inside the headline.
The page uses large neutral white background bands for the middle sections, which breaks the black-led TikTok brand feel.
The three cards under “Most ads get skipped...” use dark gray surfaces instead of approved black/white/brand-color blocks.
The small pill labels like “TikTok for Business” and “The problem” sit on charcoal gray fills rather than approved brand colors or black/white.
Body copy in the hero appears in a muted gray rather than clean white, weakening contrast and brand sharpness on black.
The outlined secondary CTA in the hero reads with a soft gray stroke/text instead of a crisp approved palette treatment, so it looks off-brand and less legible.
There is a large block of empty black space under the hero copy before the next section, making the top of the page feel unfinished and weakening hierarchy.
In the pink “How it works” section, the tiny step numbers on the black cards are too faint and small to read clearly at a glance.

findings: The three feature cards on the black section use dark grey fills instead of the approved black/white/brand-color palette, which makes the UI look off-brand immediately.
The section label pills ('Why Most Videos Miss' and 'How It Works') are rendered as charcoal chips rather than clear brand-color or black/white treatments, adding more off-brand neutral surfaces.
Secondary copy in the black section is a muted grey on black, reducing contrast and making the paragraphs harder to read at a glance.
Secondary copy inside the dark feature cards is too dim against the grey card fill, hurting legibility in the smallest text on the page.
The hero leaves a very large empty white void on the right/top, so the page opens feeling unfinished instead of bold and intentionally composed.
The pink 'How It Works' section starts with an oversized empty color block before the content, which reads like a layout break and weakens hierarchy.
The CTA label 'Start Creating' is visually polished but semantically generic, so it reads like placeholder marketing copy instead of a direct business outcome.
The headline emphasis word 'Magic' feels hypey and vague on a business landing page, which undercuts the direct, confident brand tone visible elsewhere.

findings: The rocket emoji in the hero headline introduces an extra multicolor accent and makes the headline feel off-system instead of strictly brand-led.

The headline phrase 'Turn Views Into Wins' is generic and slogan-like, which makes the top message feel less distinctive and more like placeholder campaign copy.
The page leans heavily on neutral black-and-white blocks, especially in the oversized hero and white middle section, so the brand color presence feels too sparse for TikTok’s bold, playful energy.
The card row in the white section feels visually cramped against the long paragraph above it, so the section reads dense instead of punchy and easy to scan.
The hero leaves a very large empty black void under the copy, making the top section feel unfinished and weakening the page’s primary hierarchy.
The second section is a massive plain white slab that breaks the bold TikTok feel and reads as a generic neutral background block.
The small label pills use dark grey fills instead of the approved black, white, or brand colors, which makes them feel off-brand.
The dark cards in the white section appear charcoal/grey rather than true black, introducing extra neutral surfaces and dulling the brand contrast.
The lighter grey body copy in the hero has weaker contrast than the surrounding white text, reducing legibility on the black background.
The rocket emoji in the hero headline makes the headline feel like placeholder/social copy rather than a disciplined brand wordmark treatment.
The outlined secondary CTA in the hero is visually too weak next to the filled primary CTA, so the action hierarchy feels lopsided and underdesigned.
The tiny step numbers at the top-left of the pink cards are too small and low-emphasis to function as clear section navigation cues.

findings: The hero has a large block of empty black space above the headline, making the first screen feel unfinished and weakening the page’s main visual hook.
The small 'TikTok for Business' pill is isolated far from the headline and CTAs, so it reads like a floating label instead of part of a clear hero hierarchy.
The hero subhead runs as a long single line, which hurts scanability and legibility against the oversized headline.
The dark pill and card fills appear charcoal rather than approved black, introducing off-brand neutral surfaces across the page.
The white section feels generic and editorially flat because it relies on large neutral fields instead of a stronger TikTok brand-colored composition.
The three problem cards are visually heavy, with large dark blocks that overpower their short copy and make the section feel clunky.

The 'How it works' section uses the brand pink as a full-bleed slab, but the content below it falls back into the same dark-card pattern, which makes the brand expression feel repetitive instead of playful.
The page shown has almost no visible TikTok-specific visual energy or creative device beyond color, so the tone reads more like a generic landing page than a bold creator-first brand page.

events.doing: auditing the page against brand rules

findings: The hero opens with a very large empty black void above and to the right of the content, which makes the page feel unfinished and pushes the main message too low instead of creating an immediate branded impact.
The small section-label pills read as dark grey rather than true black or a brand color, introducing off-brand neutral surfaces in multiple places.
The three problem cards use dark grey fills on the white section instead of approved brand colors or clean black/white treatment, making the block feel off-brand and visually muddy.
Body copy inside the dark cards is too small relative to the card size and breaks into awkward short lines, hurting legibility at a glance.
The hero subcopy stretches too wide across the black field, which weakens hierarchy and makes the supporting message harder to scan quickly.
The overall page tone is visually too austere for TikTok: large black and white slabs dominate while playful brand energy is underused, so the page reads more corporate than creative.
The hero has a large empty black void above the content, making the page feel unfinished and weakening the first-screen hierarchy.
The small 'TikTok for Business' pill uses a muddy dark-grey fill instead of a crisp approved brand surface, which reads off-brand against the black hero.
The three cards in 'The problem' section are filled with charcoal grey rather than approved black/white brand fields, creating an off-brand neutral UI look.
The secondary cyan CTA in the hero is as visually loud as the primary pink CTA, so the call-to-action hierarchy is unclear.
Body copy inside the dark cards is too small and cramped for the card width, reducing legibility.
